rationale: This page provides detailed constitutional and procedural information regarding the Vice President of India and the Speaker of the Lok Sabha. It covers Articles 64-68 for the Vice President and Articles 93-94 for the Speaker, including election, qualifications, term, and removal processes, making it highly suitable for generating multiple-choice questions on Indian Polity.
cleanedText:
Article 64: Vice President shall be ex officio chairman of Council of States (Rajya Sabha).
Article 65: Act as President or to discharge his functions during casual vacancies in office, or during absence of President.
Article 66: Election:
- Elected by members of an electoral college which consist members of both Houses of Parliament (both elected and nominated members).
- System of proportional representation by means of single transferable vote and secret ballot.
- Value of every vote cast in Vice-Presidential election has same value i.e. 1.
Qualifications:
- Citizen of India.
- 35 years Age.
- He is qualified for election as a member of the Council of States (Rajya Sabha).
- Must not hold any office of profit.
Article 67:
- Hold office for a term of five years.
- Resign by submitting a written resignation to President.
- Removed by Rajya Sabha resolution passed by a majority of its total membership and approved by Lok Sabha.
- Continues in office until a successor assumes charge, even after term expiry.
Article 68: An election to fill a vacancy in the office of Vice-President occurring because of his death, resignation or removal shall be held as soon as possible.
1.4. SPEAKER OF LOK SABHA
Why in the news?
Lok Sabha Speaker has nominated a Member of Parliament (MP) as an empanelled member to preside over House proceedings.
More on News
- Section 9 in Rules of Procedure and Conduct of Business in Lok Sabha:
- At start of a session or as needed, Speaker nominates up to 10 MPs as a panel of Chairpersons.
- Any of them may preside over House in absence of Speaker and Deputy Speaker.
About Office of Speaker
- Article 93: Lok Sabha chooses two of its members as Speaker and Deputy Speaker by simple majority of members present and voting on a date fixed by President.
- Position: Presiding officer, Head and representative of Lok Sabha.
- Term of Office:
- Does not vacate office until immediately before first meeting of Lok Sabha after dissolution.
- Eligible for re-election.
- Removal (Article 94): By a resolution passed by majority of all the then members of House, provided a minimum of fourteen days' notice has been given before moving such a resolution.
- When a resolution for Speaker’s removal is under consideration, he cannot preside at sitting of House, though he may be present.
- However, he can speak and take part in proceedings of House at such a time and vote in first instance, though not in case of an equality of votes.
